terms.def: Weighted-average time (in years) to receive a bond's cash flows; equals modified duration × (1 + y).
% price change per 1% yield move: ΔP/P ≈ −ModDur × Δy; primary interest rate sensitivity measure.
Dollar Value of 01bp — $ price change for a 1bp yield move; modified duration × price × 0.0001.
Second-order sensitivity of price to yield; accounts for the non-linearity that duration alone misses.
Pays only face value at maturity (no coupons); its duration equals its maturity — maximum duration for a given maturity.
When all yields across all maturities change by the same amount; the scenario duration assumes.

eli3: Duration measures how sensitive a bond's price is to changes in interest rates. A bond with duration 7 years will fall in price by approximately 7% if interest rates rise by 1%. Duration is both a risk measure and a time measure — it's the weighted average time until you receive your cash flows.

Think of duration like a balance point on a see-saw. Short-maturity bonds have low duration (the payments come quickly, so a rate change doesn't affect them much). Long-maturity zero-coupon bonds have the highest duration (all the value is in one payment far in the future).

Duration is the primary tool for managing interest rate risk in fixed income portfolios. A pension fund that needs to pay liabilities in 15 years holds a portfolio with duration close to 15 — so that if rates change, the assets and liabilities change by similar amounts.

whenWhy.title: Measuring and managing interest rate risk
whenWhy.body: Duration is the first-order measure of interest rate risk. ΔP/P ≈ -D × Δy, where D is modified duration and Δy is the yield change. A portfolio with duration 5 loses approximately 5% of its value for a 1% rise in rates. This allows portfolio managers to express their interest rate risk precisely: 'We are running 7 years of duration' communicates the interest rate sensitivity clearly. Duration management is how bond portfolio managers implement their macro views: lengthen duration when you expect rates to fall (price appreciation); shorten duration when you expect rates to rise (capital preservation).

whenWhy.title: Liability-driven investing and duration matching
whenWhy.body: Pension funds, insurance companies, and endowments have long-dated liabilities whose present value changes with interest rates. Duration matching (immunisation): match the duration of assets to the duration of liabilities. If assets and liabilities have the same duration, a parallel shift in the yield curve affects both equally — the surplus (assets minus liabilities) is protected. Defined benefit pension plans increasingly use LDI (liability-driven investing): dedicate a large portion of assets to long-duration bonds that match the duration of pension obligations, while the remaining assets pursue return-seeking strategies. This strategy became standard in the UK after the 2004 Pension Act.

whenWhy.title: DV01 for risk aggregation and hedging
whenWhy.body: DV01 (Dollar Value of a basis point, also called PV01 or PVBP): DV01 = -ΔP for a 1 basis point (0.01%) rise in yield = (Modified Duration × Price × 0.0001). DV01 expresses interest rate risk in dollar terms regardless of position size or bond price. Aggregating risk across a portfolio: portfolio DV01 = Σ(DV01_i × position_i). Hedging: to reduce portfolio DV01 by $100,000, sell Treasury futures whose DV01 per contract equals $100,000 / DV01_per_futures_contract contracts. DV01 is the universal currency of fixed income risk management — it enables comparing and netting interest rate exposure across different bond types, maturities, and currencies.

deepDiveIntro: Macaulay duration (Macaulay 1938): the weighted average time until cash flows are received, weighted by the present value of each cash flow. D_Mac = [Σ_{t=1}^{T} t × PV(C_t)] / P, where PV(C_t) = C_t / (1+y)^t. For a zero-coupon bond: Macaulay duration = maturity (all value is the single terminal cash flow). For a coupon bond: Macaulay duration < maturity (intermediate coupon payments shorten the average timing).

Modified duration: D_mod = D_Mac / (1 + y/m), where m = coupon payments per year (2 for semi-annual). The price sensitivity formula: ΔP ≈ -D_mod × P × Δy. Modified duration is the practical risk measure; Macaulay duration has the interpretation as a time-weighted average. For zero-coupon bonds: D_mod = T / (1 + y/m). For coupon bonds, D_mod < T. All else equal: duration increases with maturity, decreases with coupon rate, decreases with yield level.

deepDive.heading: Duration Formulas and Properties
deepDive.body: Macaulay duration: D = Σ_{t=1}^{T} [t × C/(1+y)^t + T × F/(1+y)^T] / P. Modified duration: D_mod = D / (1+y) for annual coupon; D/(1+y/2) for semi-annual. Properties: (1) Zero-coupon bond: D = T (maturity); D_mod = T/(1+y); (2) Coupon bond: D < T; higher coupon → lower D (more weight on early cash flows); (3) Perpetuity (consol bond): D = (1+y)/y (e.g., at y=5%: D = 21 years); (4) Portfolio duration: D_P = Σ w_i D_i (weighted average of individual durations); (5) Dollar duration: D$ = D_mod × P / 100; DV01 = D$ × 0.01 / 100 = D_mod × P × 0.0001. Duration is approximately linear in maturity for bonds of the same coupon; the relationship is non-linear (convex). [1]

deepDive.heading: Key Rate Duration and Non-Parallel Shifts
deepDive.body: Modified duration approximates the price change for a parallel shift in the yield curve (all maturities change by the same amount). In reality, the yield curve changes in non-parallel ways: steepening (long rates rise more than short), flattening (short rates rise more than long), twisting. Key rate duration (KRD): sensitivity to changes at specific points on the yield curve (1yr, 2yr, 5yr, 10yr, 30yr), holding all other rates constant. KRD_k = -∂P/∂y_k / P. Portfolio key rate durations: Σ KRD_k ≈ D_mod (sum across key rate tenors ≈ total modified duration). KRDs enable hedging non-parallel yield curve shifts: if you have high 10yr KRD and low 30yr KRD, you're positioned for flattening (10yr yields falling, 30yr rising). [2]

deepDive.heading: Duration of Floating Rate Bonds and Structured Products
deepDive.body: Floating rate notes (FRNs): coupon resets periodically to market rate (e.g., 3-month LIBOR/SOFR + spread). At each coupon reset date, the bond reprices to par — duration ≈ time to next reset. For a quarterly-reset FRN: duration ≈ 0.25 years (three months). This means FRNs have very low interest rate risk — they're not sensitive to yield curve changes. This is why FRNs are used for short-term cash management and as collateral in repo markets. Mortgage-backed securities (MBS): effective duration is complex because prepayments (homeowners refinancing when rates fall) shorten the maturity when rates fall (prepayments accelerate), creating negative convexity. MBS duration extends when rates rise (fewer prepayments) and shortens when rates fall (more prepayments), the opposite of what a portfolio manager wants. [1]

takeaways: Modified duration: ΔP/P ≈ -D_mod × Δy. A 1% rate rise on a 7yr duration bond → ~7% price loss. [1]
Macaulay duration: weighted average time to cash flows; D_mod = D_Mac / (1+y/m). [1]
DV01: price change per 1bp yield move in $ terms = D_mod × P × 0.0001. Universal risk aggregation currency. [1]
Portfolio duration = Σ w_i D_i (linear in weights). [1]
Key rate durations: sensitivity to specific yield curve tenors — enables hedging non-parallel shifts. [2]

edgeCases.q: Why doesn't duration give an exact price change prediction?
edgeCases.a: Duration gives a linear (first-order) approximation of price-yield sensitivity: ΔP ≈ -D_mod × P × Δy. The actual price-yield relationship is curved (convex), not linear. The error term is captured by convexity (the second-order correction): ΔP ≈ -D_mod × P × Δy + (1/2) × Convexity × P × (Δy)². For small yield changes (< 50bp), duration is accurate. For large changes (> 100bp), convexity matters significantly. Example: a bond with D_mod = 7 and Convexity = 100, for a 200bp (2%) rate change: Duration component: -7 × 0.02 = -14%; Convexity adjustment: 0.5 × 100 × 0.04 = +2%. Net predicted price change: -12%. Without the convexity adjustment, you'd predict -14% — a meaningful error for a 200bp move. Duration is a local (infinitesimal) approximation; convexity extends its accuracy over larger moves.

edgeCases.q: What is 'duration mismatch' and why is it dangerous for banks?
edgeCases.a: Duration mismatch: a bank's assets have higher duration than its liabilities. Classic bank business model: borrow short-term (deposits, maturity ≈ 0-1 year, low duration) and lend long-term (30yr mortgages, duration ~7 years) — earning the maturity premium (term spread). Interest rate risk: if rates rise, the bank's assets fall in value more than its liabilities (because assets have higher duration). For a deposit-funded bank that holds its bonds to maturity and doesn't mark them to market, this creates unrealised losses that aren't immediately visible. Silicon Valley Bank (SVB) collapse (March 2023): SVB invested deposit inflows in long-duration MBS and Treasuries (duration ~6-7 years) funded by short-term deposits (duration ≈ 0). When rates rose 5% in 2022, SVB's bond portfolio had massive unrealised losses (~$16B on $120B portfolio). When depositors started withdrawing (bank run), SVB had to sell bonds at a loss to meet redemptions, crystallising the duration mismatch into realised losses. SVB's failure is the canonical recent example of duration mismatch risk.

sources.label: Macaulay, F.R. (1938) — Some Theoretical Problems Suggested by the Movements of Interest Rates, Bond Yields, and Stock Prices in the United States Since 1856, NBER
Fabozzi, F.J. — Fixed Income Mathematics, 4th Edition, McGraw-Hill (2006)
